turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Niver, the State conceded there

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that the question then is whether there's anything illegal about an attempted seizure?

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: That's exactly the case, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Anything illegal about an attempted seizure without articulable suspicion.

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And is it not rather clear that that should be illegal?

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: Your Honor, our position is to the contrary. Our position is it does not violate the Fourth Amendment until there is actual restraint.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So the exclusionary rule should not apply to attempted illegal conduct by the police?

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: It does not violate — that's right, Your Honor. It does not violate this person's right under the Fourth Amendment until he is actually seized. An attempted seizure is not mentioned in the language of the Fourth Amendment or in this Court's cases.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it seems to me that you have to have — you certainly are going to defend — you're certainly going to say that the officer properly seized him when he finally did --

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- physically, and the reason is, I suppose, that he had thrown the rock away.

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: After the running began, the running by itself, the court of appeal found, was not sufficient to detain him. We have not challenged that in this case, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Niver, what about — well, there were other drugs admitted in evidence, were there not?

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: They were — there is — no, Your Honor. They were not admitted against him for the finding of the wardship. The only evidence used against him to establish wardship was the single rock of cocaine which he discarded at the time he confronted the officer. There was a bag of 15 rocks, but they were never found to be either possessed by him or in fact even cocaine. The only question before this Court was the legality of the seizure of the single rock. Continuing on the matter of the elements of a seizure, this Court has acknowledged, has defined a seizure in its case law as composed of two distinct elements: some official action and restraint upon the movement of the citizen at whom the action is directed. That has been the rule since Terry v. Ohio defined a seizure as physical force or show of authority which has restrained the liberty of a citizen. Since Terry this Court has found -- had occasion to define the parameters of both elements.

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: It would be a display of authority, certainly. It would not be a violation of the Fourth Amendment in the absent of restraint.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In your view, when the police officer puts on red lights behind a car, when does the stop take place? When the red lights are put on or when the car pulls over to the curb?

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: When the car pulls over to the curb.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And if there is no basis for the stop, suppose the car pulls away at a high speed?

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: Then the seizure has terminated.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Can the police officer acquire a basis for stopping the car after it pulls away at a high speed, if he had no basis to turn on the lights to begin with?

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: I'm not sure I understand, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Suppose the police officer has no basis for turning on the red lights, but does so anyway.

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The car then pulls away at a high speed. Is the police officer justified in pursuing and stopping?

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: I would say that if there are other circumstances present, certainly.

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: At the — well, he was ordered to drop his weapon.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: His act in dropping the weapon was a submission to the officer's authority.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And so — does that mean there was a seizure or was there not a seizure?

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: A seizure — there was a seizure of the person and there was a seizure of his weapon.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even though he never caught him? Even though he never subdued him?

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: The seizure ended when he ran away and was not — when he ran from the control of the officer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But he was never under the control of the officer, under your argument as I understand it.

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: Our argument is that if a person submits to a show of — submits to a show of authority —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: — at that point he is seized. It doesn't require physical control, not in the sense that the officer and the person are standing next to each other. It requires submission. If a person objectively submits to the show of authority by a police officer, that is a detention within the meaning of the Fourth Amendment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So if this man understood the chase to be a show of authority where he was attempting to catch him, then it would be a seizure and it would have been an illegal seizure? And throwing it away would be just like throwing the gun away?

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: In this case — if I understand Your Honor's question, in this case it was not a submission because he attempted to run.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, my fellow threw the gun on the ground and then ran, and he never got caught.

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: He was ordered to do so, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So the thing that's wrong here is there is no — nothing equivalent to an order to stop or an order to throw the rock away?

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: That's exactly right, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Niver, you're saying, if I understand it, that there's no Fourth Amendment violation when the police obtain evidence as a result of unlawful conduct by them, unless that unlawful conduct constitutes a completed seizure? Is that fair to say?

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: Or unless the police officer has ordered the person to —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yeah, but that in your view would amount to a seizure, because it would be a submission to authority, right?

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: Correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Okay. Would --

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: It's very much — it's —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right, would your — would your position be different if instead of raising the issue under the Fourth Amendment, the issue had been raised under the Due Process Clause, pure and simple?

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: If the respondent had argued --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Somebody says there's a violation of due process in obtaining evidence as a result of unlawful police conduct and using that evidence in trial.

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: Well, I believe that this Court has not so held since Roshan. I don't believe — no, I don't believe that a motion to suppress under the Due Process Clause would be -- would be cognizable. Unless it violates the Fourth Amendment, the evidence is admissable.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that for evidentiary purposes, illegal police conduct is really not subject to regulation unless you do have a narrow or strict Fourth Amendment violation?

turns.name: RONALD E. NIVER
turns.text: Well, not all intrusive police conduct violates the Constitution, and if it does violate the Constitution, it does not necessarily violate the Fourth Amendment. To be suppressible, the police conduct has to violate the Fourth Amendment. At the time of the of clarification in your hypothetical, Justice Stevens. Does the individual stop at all, or is he running the entire time and throws the gun away?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He's running the entire time.

turns.name: CLIFFORD M. SLOAN
turns.text: If he's running the entire time, I think there is not a seizure of the person. There is a seizure of the gun, just as in this case the respondent was running the entire time, and there is not a seizure when he is running. If he stops, then it's acquiescence to the authority. But if he's running the entire time there is not a seizure of the person.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did I understand you to say the procedure was taking place? Did you say the — the arrest was taking place when he was running?

turns.name: CLIFFORD M. SLOAN
turns.text: I — no, I did not say that, Your Honor. I said that — in fact it's quite clear that it's our position that the seizure did not take place until the officer actually gained control of the individual. Until that —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what was going on during the running?

turns.name: CLIFFORD M. SLOAN
turns.text: Well, what the record reveals is that Officer Pertoso was running up a street in one direction, respondent was running —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did he tell him he was an officer of acquiescence --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, he threw his gun on the ground pursuant to the command —

turns.name: CLIFFORD M. SLOAN
turns.text: Well, I don't think that the throwing —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: — and then ran.

turns.name: CLIFFORD M. SLOAN
turns.text: I think that the throwing of the object is distinct from the question of the seizure of the person. In one instance you're talking about the seizure of the person.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: CLIFFORD M. SLOAN
turns.text: There is clearly a seizure of the gun in that instance. Now as to whether the person has been seized it would be a close factual question in that situation whether he had manifested acquiescence. If he held up his hands and said you've got me, or something —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, my facts are he just threw the gun on the ground and ran, and was never caught.

turns.name: CLIFFORD M. SLOAN
turns.text: Well, in those — under those facts I do not think that it would be a seizure, because I don't think that there is anything, aside from the throwing down of the gun, that manifests acquiescence in the coercive assertion of authority for the seizure of the person. The mere throwing down of an object is not itself sufficient to establish acquiescence in the authority. 2 authority. He said -- the police officer without any consequence of that officer bearing down on him. If that officer wouldn't have been there blocking his path —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you're arguing for this position that Justice Kennedy just stated right now.

turns.name: JAMES L. LOZENSKI
turns.text: Well, maybe if Justice Kennedy would ask his question again, or propose his hypothetical — there's another element here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You're arguing that the police, by beginning to chase, have automatically granted immunity from introducing the evidence, because he either — one, he can run away and be successful, or two, he can go to the officer and say you have made a mistake, here's the cocaine but you can't introduce it.

turns.name: JAMES L. LOZENSKI
turns.text: Well, I don't think either one of those is the case. I think Hodari panicked. And I think, as a lot of these cases indicate, that when officers bear down with this show of authority like this, they will panic and discard the contraband. And I think that is exactly what happened here. I don't think Hodari gave thought to anything, because this all happened so fast in this 11-foot scenario. And I think he panicked and abandoned the contraband, without any reflection on his part.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, do you take the position that the word "seizure" in the Fourth Amendment includes 2 MR. LOZENSKI: No, I don't believe that is the

turns.name: JAMES L. LOZENSKI
turns.text: Well, if he escapes from the police officer he is no longer seized, Your Honor. But if the police officer is chasing him and there is a sufficient show of authority, some imitating and -- some intimidating course of conduct, yes., I believe that that person has been seized.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that, that — you see a scenario, policeman running down the street and the suspect running ahead of him, and the policeman shouting stop, stop, thief. That has been a seizure, while he -r-

turns.name: JAMES L. LOZENSKI
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even while he's running he is seized?

turns.name: JAMES L. LOZENSKI
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But at a certain point if he starts running faster than the officer and gets away, the seizure ceases?

turns.name: JAMES L. LOZENSKI
turns.text: Yes. If he gets away from the officer I believe that the seizure terminates.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How do we determine when it terminated?

turns.name: JAMES L. LOZENSKI
turns.text: When he gets away from the officer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: A certain number of feet, or something like that?
